title: Hub’s New European Bauhaus Festival Satellite Event highlights cultural heritage-led solutions to Europe’s housing and climate challenges
content:
The European Heritage Hub’s official satellite event of the New European Bauhaus Festival, “Cultural Heritage as a Resource for Energy-Efficient and Inclusive Renovation and Affordable Housing Policies”, brought together policymakers, heritage experts, architects, local authorities and finance professionals to explore how cultural heritage can contribute to energy-efficient renovation, affordable housing and climate resilience.

The event included the participation of Hub consortium partners Eurocities and ICLEI, and members of the European Heritage Alliance, including the Architects’ Council of Europe (ACE) and the International Council on Monuments and Sites (ICOMOS). It built on the Hub’s December 2025 Heritage Dialogues webinar,  “Cultural Heritage and Building Renovation: Aligning the EPBD and Affordable Housing Objectives”.

creator: Liv Martin

title: When Heritage Still Breathes: What European Heritage Debates Can Learn from the Southern Mediterranean
content:
In many policy conversations, heritage is treated as a sector: something to be funded, digitised, displayed, or protected. In much of the Southern Mediterranean, it is still something more intimate. Rather than only an object standing behind glass, or a record surviving in official registers, heritage is what people use to make sense of place, hardship, memory, and change.

This matters for Europe, not because Europe lacks living heritage, and not because a simple North/South divide can explain cultural experience. Europe also contains many internal margins, rural worlds, regional memories, and migrant histories where heritage remains deeply lived. The point is more precise: Southern Mediterranean experience can remind European heritage debates that institutional recognition is never enough when social attachment is weak.
snippet: In many policy conversations, heritage is treated as a sector: something to be funded, digitised, displayed, or protected. In much of the Southern Mediterranean, it is still something more intimate.
creator: Liv Martin

title: Shall We Go to the Forest and See What We Find?
content: Finland. The country of thousands of lakes, and even more forests. Those green and sacred places that provide the backdrop for many Finnish folk traditions, such as the adventures of mythical characters in the national epic, Kalevala. Forests are a core point of Finnish identity. Forests, the favourite place for many Finnish people to spend their summer in, often in wooden cabins passed down through centuries from parent to child, cousin to cousin. But what if you cannot go to a summer cottage, or on a hiking trip to a national park? What if all you have is one afternoon? Will forests be changed to private green courtyards, with only memories of the past?

title: Friction at the Human Scale: Cycling, Moral Conflict and Behavioural Change in Lisbon
content: Why does behavioural change remain fragile and contested even when infrastructure expands? In Lisbon, cycling is far from being seamlessly adopted; it is learned, negotiated, and morally contested. As the city undergoes a complex transition toward sustainable mobility, cycling has emerged not only as an infrastructural solution but as an embodied, cultural, and politically charged urban practice. Far from being a neutral technical adjustment, the expansion of cycling infrastructure has intensified debates over who the city is for, how streets should be shared, and which forms of mobility are legitimate. These tensions echo broader questions across Europe about how to reconcile tradition and change, individual freedom and collective responsibility, and competing visions of urban life.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Hub joins global partners to launch Heritage Adapts! campaign
content: Heritage Adapts! launches today as the first global campaign uniting the heritage sector behind a shared mission: at least 3,000 sites and practices taking locally led climate adaptation action by 2030. Spearheaded by a coalition led by Preserving Legacies, an initiative supported by the National Geographic Society, and its strategic partner the EU-funded European Heritage Hub (including Project Leader Europa Nostra and partner ICLEI Europe), among others, the campaign mobilises local stewards behind a collective pledge and provides the technical guidance, localised data access, and global connections to help them succeed. Recognised by the U.N.’s climate agency as a Plan to Accelerate Solutions advancing the Global Goal on Adaptation, Heritage Adapts! connects local action to global impact, strengthening community resilience worldwide.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Fifth Hub Forum highlights heritage as a driver of peace and cooperation across the Mediterranean
content:
How can cultural heritage contribute to peace in a region marked by conflict, displacement and political division? This question guided the fifth European Heritage Hub Forum, “Mare Nostrum: Fostering Peace through Heritage,” held on 29 May 2026 at the CYENS – Centre of Excellence in Nicosia as part of the European Cultural Heritage Summit 2026, hosted by Europa Nostra and the Europa Nostra Heritage Hub in Nicosia.

Organised within the framework of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub and with the support of the European Union and the Cultural Protection Fund of the British Council, the Forum brought together policymakers, heritage professionals, academics and civil society leaders from across Europe and the Mediterranean. Throughout the afternoon, participants explored how cultural heritage can serve as a catalyst for reconciliation, social cohesion and cross-border cooperation.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Second “In Varietate Concordia” Talk uses history as a backdrop for inspiring hope, wonder and mutual respect in a fragmented world
content:
On 26 May 2026, the second In Varietate Concordia Talk, entitled “Heritage as the Soul of Mare Nostrum”, gathered award-winning historian, author and broadcaster Prof. Bettany Hughes and Prof. Dr. Hermann Parzinger, archaeologist and Executive President of Europa Nostra. Hosted in Nicosia, Europe’s last divided capital, the event drew on the panelists’ historical knowledge and touched upon themes of rising nationalism, misinformation and the destruction of cultural heritage, as well as hospitality, harmony, wonder, democracy, peace and shared memory.

The dialogue took place at the Leventis Municipal Museum of Nicosia, in the framework of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub and the European Cultural Heritage Summit 2026. Sneška Quaedvlieg-Mihailović, Secretary General of Europa Nostra and Project Leader of the European Heritage Hub, moderated the conversation.

title: Join the Hub’s New European Bauhaus Festival satellite event on cultural heritage as a driver for energy-efficient and inclusive renovation
content:
The EU-funded European Heritage Hub will host a satellite event in Brussels and via livestream on 11 June 2026 during the Festival of the New European Bauhaus, exploring how heritage-led approaches contribute to sustainability, aesthetics and inclusion during a time when climate, energy and housing crises are converging.

It will bring together EU institutions, local and regional authorities, civil society organisations, architects, heritage professionals, financial actors and practitioners to examine how heritage-led approaches can contribute to the transformation of Europe’s built environment in the context of climate, energy and housing challenges.

The event is organised in the frame of the European Heritage Hub, with contributions from the consortium partners Eurocities and ICLEI, and members of the European Heritage Alliance including the Architects’ Council of Europe (ACE) and the International Council on Monuments and Sites (ICOMOS).

The event aims to contribute to advancing the New European Bauhaus’ vision of beautiful, sustainable and inclusive living environments, positioning cultural heritage as a strategic resource for Europe’s housing and climate transition.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Second “In Varietate Concordia” Talk to take place in Nicosia, featuring guests Bettany Hughes and Hermann Parzinger
content:
The second In Varietate Concordia Talk, “Heritage as the Soul of Mare Nostrum”, will take place on 26 May 2026 (18:00 EEST / 17:00 CEST) at the Leventis Municipal Museum of Nicosia, Cyprus as part of the European Cultural Heritage Summit 2026. A recording will be made available on the European Heritage Hub’s YouTube channel following the event.

The Talk features a high-level dialogue between two of Europe’s most prominent voices in history and archaeology: award-winning historian, author and broadcaster Prof. Bettany Hughes, and Prof. Dr. Hermann Parzinger, celebrated archaeologist and Executive President of Europa Nostra. The discussion will be moderated by Sneška Quaedvlieg-Mihailović, Secretary General of Europa Nostra and Project Leader of the European Heritage Hub.

creator: Liv Martin

title: European Heritage Hub Advisory Council holds first meeting of the Preparatory Action
content:
The third meeting of the European Heritage Hub’s Advisory Council was held online on 5 May 2026, marking the first meeting organised since the start of the Hub’s Preparatory Action phase in September 2025. Chaired by Prof. Dr. Hermann Parzinger, Executive President of Europa Nostra, the meeting brought together both current and new members, reflecting the broad expertise, diversity and growing ambition of the Hub at a key moment in its development and mission to contribute to Europe’s green, digital and social transformation. 

The meeting provided an opportunity to reflect on recent EU policy developments, particularly the recently published Culture Compass for Europe and the proposal for the next Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF), especially the AgoraEU funding programme, both seen as crucial for securing stronger support for culture and cultural heritage in the years ahead.
